intent: Auto-reload my systemd services whenever I push to the repo
finding: Across three different repos the user asked for the same pattern: when a push lands, the corresponding systemd/user service should reload automatically so they don't have to restart it by hand. In whisper-writer the user said 'Can you make it so that it auto-reloads whenever you push, just like the Autodoro project?' — pointing at the autodoro post-push hook as a template. In autodoro itself they added it ad-hoc ('actually, add onpush hook that reloads the service'). In the ecc/continuous-learning context they asked for a weekly auto-pull. The outcome the user is pursuing is 'keep running services current with my source without manual restart'. It recurs across repos (whisper-writer, autodoro, ecc, and implicitly any future service-backed project). A single durable artifact (a reusable post-push-reload skill or hook template parameterised by repo/service-name) could replace the manual copy-paste of the autodoro hook into each new project.
surface_of_recurrence: Across repos and across time — every time the user sets up a new local service-backed project they re-ask for the same hook
existing_mitigation: autodoro has a per-project post-push hook; this has not been generalised into a reusable skill or template. The user has explicitly pointed at autodoro as the pattern to replicate, which is the strongest signal that generalisation would help.

intent: Keep my NixOS config mirrored with manual changes I make to Mint
finding: In the nixos-config session the user said 'I keep doing things to mint. How can I make sure these changes are mirrored?' The outcome they want is 'my declarative NixOS config doesn't silently drift from the imperative Mint system I'm actually living on'. This recurs by nature — every package install, service toggle, or config edit on Mint is a potential drift event, and the user has been accumulating them. A durable artifact (a scheduled drift-analyzer run that diffs Mint state against the nix config and surfaces a list of candidate mirrorings) could replace the manual 'I need to remember to port this over' loop. The three-question test passes: outcome (no drift), recurrence (continuous), single artifact (scheduled drift-analyzer + report).
surface_of_recurrence: Continuous — every time the user touches Mint imperatively
existing_mitigation: A drift-analyzer exists but appears to be run ad-hoc as a programmatic invocation rather than on a schedule. Not wired into a recurring job.

intent: Keep vendored upstream forks auto-pulled with a diff for review
finding: The user has token-optimizer vendored as a fork and set up a cron that pulls upstream and surfaces the diff for review. In a separate session on ecc/continuous-learning they asked for the same pattern ('ecc auto git pull weekly'). The outcome is 'stay close to upstream on forks without manually remembering to pull each one'. Recurrence: any time a new upstream-tracked fork enters the user's environment, the same wiring needs to happen. A single durable artifact (a generalised 'vendored-fork-watcher' skill or cron template that takes a fork path + upstream remote + cadence) could replace the per-fork one-off wiring.
surface_of_recurrence: Per-fork, across time — each new vendored dependency is a new instance
existing_mitigation: A per-project cron exists for token-optimizer. It has not been generalised into a template or skill that the user can point at a new fork and have configured in one step.

intent: Keep disk space in check without manual cleanup sessions
finding: The user opened a session with 'The disk is super packed. What are the big memory hogs?' and proceeded through a manual investigation + cleanup loop. The outcome is 'disk doesn't fill up to the point where I have to drop what I'm doing and go hunt for hogs'. Recurrence is by physics — disks fill up, caches grow, old node_modules and nix store generations accumulate. A single durable artifact (a scheduled disk-report that surfaces top-N growing directories + obvious candidates like old nix generations, stale docker images, node_modules in archived repos) could convert this from a reactive fire-fight into a low-friction weekly glance.
surface_of_recurrence: Periodic — every few weeks to months as disk fills
existing_mitigation: None observed. Cleanup happens reactively and manually each time.

intent: Batch large uncommitted piles into logical commits across my repos
finding: The user opened a session with 'There's almost 200 unstaged/pushed files. Wtf' and then worked through sorting and committing them in logical groups. The outcome is 'repos don't accumulate 200-file uncommitted drifts between focused sessions'. Recurrence appears to span at least dotfiles and adjacent personal repos. A durable artifact already exists for dotfiles (sync-agent.sh) but is scoped to that one repo. Generalising it into a skill that can be pointed at any repo and proposes logical commit groupings would cover the pattern across the user's broader set of personal repos. Noting per categories.md that this boundary between 'recurs' and 'one-off' is fuzzy; writing it and letting downstream review decide.
surface_of_recurrence: Across the user's personal repos, periodically
existing_mitigation: dotfiles sync-agent.sh handles this for the dotfiles repo. Not generalised beyond that one repo.
